meta.title: W639 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes Viano W639 engine — OM646, OM651, OM642, M112, M272. Real UK costs, honest reliability data. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes Viano W639 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W639 engine typically costs £2,800–£7,500 supply only, depending on the engine — the OM646/OM651 four-cylinder diesels sit at £2,800–£5,000, while the OM642 V6 diesel ranges from £4,000–£6,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes Viano W639 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651.940 four-cylinder diesel (2.2 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY, 2010–2014) and the M112 V6 petrol (3.2/3.7) are the most dependable. The OM651 is a refined, efficient, and reliable engine that replaced the older OM646. The M112 is one of Mercedes' most durable petrol V6s with no balance-shaft issues. The OM642 V6 diesel is also robust but has known oil cooler seal issues.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes Viano W639?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM646 timing chain tensioner failure (pre-facelift diesels), OM646 injector seal failure ('Black Death'), OM642 oil cooler seal leaks (V6 diesel), M272 balance shaft failure (pre-2008 3.5 petrol), and — unrelated to the engine — a recall for fuel system issues on 2003–2006 models.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes Viano W639 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and the year. The OM651 diesel (2010–2014) and M112 V6 petrol are genuinely robust. The OM646 pre-facelift diesel has known timing-chain and injector issues — budget for inspection. The OM642 V6 diesel is reliable but needs oil cooler seal checks. The M272 V6 has balance-shaft issues on pre-2008 engines. Check whether the fuel system recall has been performed on 2003–2006 models.

mainEntity.name: Which W639 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but pre-facelift OM646 diesels (2.0 CDI/2.2 CDI, 2003–2010) need careful checking for timing chain history and injector seal condition. If you're buying one, listen for a rattling noise on cold start and check for diesel smell. The M272 V6 (3.5, pre-2008) also needs balance-shaft history checked.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W639 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain rattle on cold start (OM646, OM642), rough idle or diesel smell (OM646 injector failure), oil leaks around the engine valley (OM642), or balance-shaft rattle (M272) are key warnings. Also check whether the fuel system recall (2003–2006 models) has been performed. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes Viano W639 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000+ W639 (particularly a V6 or 4MATIC), a £3,000–£4,500 recon is 38–56% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £4,500 example with rust or commercial wear, a £3,000+ recon may exceed the car's worth — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line. For the OM642 V6 diesel, these engines regularly exceed 200,000 miles, so a recon is usually a once-in-a-lifetime event.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes Viano W639 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 diesel, OM642 V6 diesel and M112 V6 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The OM646 is typically good for 150,000+ miles if the timing chain has been addressed. The M272 V6 is good for 150,000+ miles if the balance shaft has been addressed. The M112 V6 is the most durable petrol option.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the Viano and Vito?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are built on the W639 platform. The Viano is the MPV/passenger version (this page) with comfort-focused interior, seating for up to 8, and higher trim levels. The Vito is the commercial van version with a more basic interior, load-carrying focus, and higher payload capacity. They share the same engines, chassis, and many components — but the Viano is designed for people transport, while the Vito is designed for goods transport. See the Vito generation page for specifics on the commercial variant.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes Viano W639 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., 2.2 CDI, 3.0 CDI, 3.5) and registration year (pre-2010 or post-2010), and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes Viano W639 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W639 MPV — from the OM646 2.2‑litre four‑cylinder diesels to the OM651 replacements, the smooth M112 and M272 V6 petrols, and the torquey OM642 V6 diesel. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the timing‑chain and balance‑shaft issues that define this generation's ownership story. Backed by real UK W639 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes Viano W639 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W639 MPV specifically — spanning both pre‑facelift (2003–2010) and facelift (2010–2014) variants, with diesel and petrol options.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM642 V6 diesel: 85% confidence — a well‑regarded engine with strong torque and good reliability, though oil cooler seal leaks affect some examples. M112 V6: 85% confidence — one of Mercedes' most durable petrol V6s, with a proven track record. OM646: 75% confidence — a reliable workhorse but known for timing‑chain tensioner wear and injector seal issues. OM651: 80% confidence — the updated four‑cylinder diesel with improved efficiency and reliability. M272 V6: 75% confidence — a smooth, powerful engine but early units suffer from balance‑shaft failure.

overview.intro: The W639 is the second‑generation Mercedes Viano, launched in July 2003 as the successor to the W638. Built in Vitoria-Gasteiz, Spain, it features rear‑wheel drive (unlike its front‑wheel‑drive predecessor), a choice of three body lengths, and either rear‑wheel or 4MATIC all‑wheel drive. The engine range spans the OM646 2.2‑litre four‑cylinder diesel (2003–2010), the OM651 2.2‑litre four‑cylinder diesel (2010–2014 facelift), the OM642 3.0‑litre V6 diesel (2006–2014), the M112 3.2‑litre and 3.7‑litre V6 petrols (2003–2008), and the M272 3.5‑litre V6 petrol (2007–2014). A 2010 facelift brought revised styling, new engines, and BlueEFFICIENCY technology.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W639 (rear‑wheel drive) • Predecessor: W638 • Successor: W447 V‑Class • Sibling model: Vito (commercial van).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W639 Viano generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM646 and OM651 diesels as these vehicles enter higher‑mileage commercial and family‑use territory.

items.quote: The 3.0‑litre V6 diesel OM642 delivers 224 PS and 440 Nm of torque — effortless, refined, and perfectly suited to the Viano's size and weight. It's a robust, proven engine that regularly exceeds 200,000 miles with proper maintenance, though oil cooler seal leaks are a known issue. The pre‑facelift 204 PS version is equally dependable.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless V6 torque and refinement for long journeys or heavy loads.

items.quote: The 3.7‑litre M112 V6 delivers 231 PS of smooth, naturally aspirated power — no turbo lag, no balance‑shaft worries, just a robust, proven V6 that keeps going. The 3.2‑litre M112 E32 is equally solid. The M112 is one of Mercedes' most durable petrol engines and a perfect match for the Viano's character.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 refinement without the M272's balance‑shaft risk.

items.quote: The 3.5‑litre M272 V6 is a great engine — smooth, powerful, and refined. But early units (roughly 2005–2008) suffer from a well‑documented balance‑shaft gear failure. The balance shaft sprocket wears prematurely, causing the timing to jump and potentially destroying the engine. If you're buying a pre‑2008 Viano 3.5, check the engine number against Mercedes' affected range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers willing to check engine numbers and budget for balance‑shaft replacement, or those buying post‑2008 examples.

items.quote: The OM642 V6 diesel is a brilliant engine — but when it fails, it fails expensively. Oil cooler seal leaks require the intake manifold to be removed, a labour‑intensive job. Timing‑chain wear can also occur on higher‑mileage examples. A full replacement can easily exceed £6,500.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who need V6 diesel torque and are prepared for higher maintenance costs.

items.quote: The 2.2‑litre OM651 diesel is a significant improvement over the OM646 — more refined, more efficient, and more reliable. With 163 PS and 360 Nm of torque, it's more than enough for the Viano's day‑to‑day duties. It's the sweet spot in the diesel range — cheaper to buy, cheaper to run, and cheaper to replace than the V6 diesel.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy and reliability without the V6's running costs.

items.quote: The 3.2‑litre M112 V6 is often overlooked in favour of the larger 3.7 or the diesels, but it offers 190 PS of smooth, reliable V6 power with significantly better fuel economy than the 3.7. It's cheaper to buy, cheaper to insure, and cheaper to replace. The M112 is bulletproof — the W639's sleeper pick.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 refinement without the fuel bills of the 3.7 or the diesel's DPF concerns.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Workhorse — reliable but needs chain attention.
V6 Diesel — torquey, refined, but pricier to maintain.
Best Petrol — bulletproof V6, no major issues.
Highest Risk — great engine, but check balance shaft.

economicsRule.title: The W639 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,000 OM651 recon against a £6,000 Viano sits at 50–83% of the car's value. On a sub‑£5,000 Viano, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £8,000+ example (particularly a V6 or 4MATIC), the same recon drops to a routine 38–63% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. For the OM642 V6 diesel, the maths is similar — but these engines regularly exceed 200,000 miles, so a recon is usually a once‑in‑a‑lifetime event.

problems.title: OM646 Timing Chain Tensioner Failure
problems.rootCause: The OM646's timing chain tensioner can fail due to inadequate oil pressure at idle, causing chain stretch and potentially catastrophic valve damage. A rattling noise on cold start is the classic early warning sign.
problems.recommendation: On any OM646‑powered W639, listen for a rattling noise on cold start — if you hear it, get the timing chain inspected immediately. A £800–£1,500 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £2,800–£4,800 replacement that follows a skipped chain.

cta.label: Compare OM646 engine prices for your W639 2.0/2.2 CDI →

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Failure
problems.rootCause: The M272 V6 has a well‑documented balance‑shaft gear failure — the sprocket wears prematurely, causing the timing to jump and potentially destroying the engine. The issue affects engines built before approximately 2008. Symptoms include a rattling noise and check engine lights.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a pre‑2008 Viano 3.5, check the engine number against Mercedes' affected range. If it's affected, budget for balance‑shaft replacement or walk away. A £1,200–£2,200 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £4,500–£7,500 replacement that follows a complete failure.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your W639 3.5 →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leaks
problems.rootCause: The OM642's oil cooler seals degrade over time, allowing oil to leak into the valley of the V6 engine. This is a labour‑intensive repair as the intake manifold must be removed to access the seals.
problems.recommendation: Check for oil leaks around the engine valley on any OM642‑powered W639 — a £800–£1,300 repair is far cheaper than the £4,000–£6,500 replacement that follows oil starvation. Also check for oil in the wiring harness — another known OM642 issue.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your W639 3.0 CDI →

problems.title: OM646 Injector Seal Failure
problems.rootCause: The OM646's injector seals can fail, allowing combustion gases to leak past — this creates carbon buildup ('Black Death') and eventually causes injector seizure and cylinder damage. A diesel smell in the cabin or rough idle are classic symptoms.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rough idle or diesel smell, get the injector seals inspected immediately — a £400–£800 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,800–£4,800 replacement that follows if ignored.

problems.title: W639 Fuel System Recall (2003–2006)
problems.affectedModels: Viano and Vito models built between March 2003 and September 2006.
problems.rootCause: Daimler issued a recall for 734 vehicles where the engine could suddenly cut out due to a fault in the fuel supply connection line.
problems.recommendation: If you own a W639 built between March 2003 and September 2006, check with a Mercedes dealer to confirm whether the fuel system recall has been performed. A sudden engine cut‑out at speed is dangerous — this is a safety‑critical recall.

cta.label: Check W639 fuel system recall status →

replacementCosts.h2: W639 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — W639 MPV shares its engine bay with the Vito commercial van, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W639 Viano MPV only. Vito commercial van shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page. The W639 was available in three body lengths — short, long, and extra‑long — with the engine range consistent across all lengths.

mostRequestedEngines: 1. OM651.940 (2.2 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY) — facelift diesel [PATTERN DATA]
3. OM642.990 (3.0 CDI) — V6 diesel choice [PATTERN DATA]
